nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in intertidal and shallow subtidal zones on rocky reefs and coral rubble across the tropical Indo-West Pacific, with Hawaii as the type locality. Animals occupy abandoned gastropod shells (especially globose Turbo and Nerita shells) and shelter in rockwork between foraging bouts.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices for shelter
sand or fine rubble substrate for sifting
an assortment of empty gastropod shells of progressively larger sizes (Turbo and Nerita shapes preferred)
mature reef tank with stable parameters
secure rockwork to prevent dislodgement during foraging

diet.notes: Generally considered an algae-grazing scavenger that consumes filamentous green algae, cyanobacteria, detritus, leftover fish food, and small invertebrates. In well-fed reef tanks with abundant algae, little supplemental feeding is required; supplementing with dried seaweed (Nori) or sinking pellets is commonly suggested once available algae are depleted to discourage predation on snails and tubeworms.

goodWith: peaceful reef fish (gobies, blennies, cardinalfish, small wrasses)
most LPS, SPS, soft corals, and zoanthids
other small reef-safe hermits and shrimp of similar size
larger snails with shells the crab cannot fit into
avoidWith: small or thin-shelled snails (commonly killed for their shells)
small feather duster worms and tubeworms (have been reported eaten)
very small ornamental shrimp and tiny inverts in densely stocked tanks
predatory wrasses, triggerfish, large puffers, and large hawkfish

summary: A small Indo-Pacific cleanup-crew hermit crab with black-and-white banded legs, blue eyestalks with orange tips, and a distinctively enlarged left claw. Generally considered a useful algae-grazing scavenger for reef tanks.
careNotes: The Zebra Hermit Crab (Calcinus laevimanus) is a small marine hermit in the family Diogenidae, widespread across the tropical Indo-West Pacific from East Africa and Madagascar through Indonesia, the Philippines, Australia, and Papua New Guinea to Hawaii. Despite the 'Hawaiian Zebra Hermit' trade name, the species is not endemic to Hawaii — it simply turns up frequently in the trade because animals are commonly collected there. The Zebra Hermit is distinguished from close relatives such as the Electric Blue Hermit (C. elegans) and the Halloween Hermit (Ciliopagurus strigatus) by its enlarged LEFT claw — most hermit crabs are right-handed — which accounts for the alternate trade name 'Left-Handed Hermit Crab'. This dominant left chela serves as a defensive door when the animal retreats into its shell and as a visual display during the shell-swapping competitions that make this species territorial toward its own kind. Although generally peaceful toward fish and corals, the Zebra Hermit tends to compete aggressively over shells and individual animals have been reported to kill snails to claim their shells. Providing an assortment of progressively larger empty gastropod shells — globose Turbo and Nerita shapes are preferred — is commonly advised to reduce this behavior and to allow animals to move into larger shells as they grow through successive molts. Like all decapod crustaceans, this species is highly sensitive to copper-based medications; exposure to copper-based treatments is generally fatal. If keeping multiple individuals, providing approximately 150 L (~40 US gal) of space per crab is commonly suggested to limit shell competition.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Zebra Hermit Crab is not considered achievable in a home aquarium, and trade animals are sourced from the wild — primarily from Hawaii. Like most marine hermits, Calcinus laevimanus has a planktonic larval phase (comprising six zoeal stages plus a glaucothoe stage — a transitional juvenile form that settles out of the water column before taking up the hermit lifestyle) that requires open-water rearing conditions not achievable in a typical display tank. In the wild, females carry approximately 3,000 eggs, with peak breeding occurring from February through October in Hawaii.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Dwarf Zebra/Orange & Black Hermit Crab
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.023-1.025), care level ('Easy'), maximum size (~1 in), diet (herbivorous scavenger), temperament ('Peaceful'), reef compatibility ('Yes'), and Indo-Pacific (Hawaii through Central Pacific) origin. The intended source URL (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/zebra-hermit-crab) returned 404; this product-detail URL is the working LiveAquaria page.

additional.name: Tank Facts — Dwarf Zebra Hermit Crab
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (76-80°F, pH 7.8-8.4, SG 1.023-1.026, dKH 8-12), diet (herbivore/scavenger on algae and cyanobacteria), reef safety, copper intolerance, and adult size (~1 in). NOTE: Tank Facts incorrectly states 'native to the Caribbean' — this is contradicted by every other source (LiveAquaria, Wikipedia, AquariumWiki) and was disregarded for nativeRange. The higher temperature ceiling (80°F) was factored into the averaged temperatureC range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Calcinus laevimanus
additional.notes: Used to confirm family (Diogenidae), order (Decapoda), correct Indo-West Pacific distribution (East Africa to Hawaii, type locality Hawaii), habitat (intertidal/shallow subtidal rocky reefs and rubble), maximum size (30 mm total length, ~3 cm), shell preference (Turbo/Nerita), reproductive biology (six zoeal stages, ~3,000 eggs), and territoriality over shells (informs semi-aggressive temperament assignment).

additional.name: ReefBay — Calcinus laevimanus Care Guide
additional.notes: Third secondary used to confirm trade synonyms ('Left-Handed Hermit Crab', 'Dwarf Zebra Hermit Crab'), diet (algae, detritus, leftover fish food, small invertebrates), care level ('Easy'), and peaceful temperament rating.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Sources do not give a typical molt interval. Adequate iodine, calcium, and trace elements are commonly cited as important for proper molting; molts are generally left in the tank to be consumed for calcium reuptake. Animals are vulnerable to predation by tankmates and to shell theft by other hermits in the hours immediately after molting.
crustacean.breedingNotes: No reliably reported captive-breeding success in the home-aquarium literature. The species has a six-stage zoeal larval phase plus a glaucothoe stage that is not practical to rear in a display tank.
